items.notes: Pronounced 'PAI-ta'. One of the most basic clothing words. Partitive: paitaa. Plural: paidat.
examples: Tämä paita on sininen. — This shirt is blue.
Ostan uuden paidan. — I will buy a new shirt.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HOU-sut'. Always plural in Finnish (like 'trousers' in British English). Singular form 'housu' is rarely used alone.
examples: Nämä housut ovat liian pitkät. — These pants are too long.
Tarvitsen uudet housut. — I need new pants.

items.notes: Pronounced 'MEK-ko'. Double 'k'. Partitive: mekkoa. Plural: mekot.
examples: Hänellä on kaunis mekko. — She has a beautiful dress.
Punainen mekko sopii sinulle. — The red dress suits you.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TAK-ki'. Double 'k'. General word for any jacket or coat. Partitive: takkia. Plural: takit.
examples: Ota takki mukaan, ulkona on kylmä. — Take a jacket, it is cold outside.
Missä minun takkini on? — Where is my jacket?

items.notes: Pronounced 'KEN-gät'. Plural form; singular 'kenkä' (one shoe). Note the consonant gradation: kenkä → kengät (nk → ng).
examples: Nämä kengät ovat mukavat. — These shoes are comfortable.
Riisu kengät eteisessä. — Take off your shoes in the hallway.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAT-tu'. Double 't'. Refers to a brimmed hat. Partitive: hattua. Plural: hatut.
examples: Hänellä on iso hattu. — He/she has a big hat.
Laita hattu päähän. — Put a hat on your head.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SU-kat'. Plural of 'sukka'. Consonant gradation: sukka → sukat (kk → k).
examples: Missä ovat puhtaat sukat? — Where are the clean socks?
Tarvitsen lämpimät sukat. — I need warm socks.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAN-sik-kaat'. Plural of 'hansikas'. Refers to fingered gloves specifically. Compare with 'käsineet' (mittens).
examples: Laita hansikkaat käsiin. — Put gloves on your hands.
Unohdin hansikkaani kotiin. — I forgot my gloves at home.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HUI-vi'. Can mean a scarf or a headscarf. Partitive: huivia. Plural: huivit.
examples: Huivi pitää kaulan lämpimänä. — A scarf keeps the neck warm.
Ostan villahuivin. — I will buy a wool scarf.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SAAP-paat'. Plural of 'saapas'. Essential in Finnish winters. Consonant gradation: saapas → saappaat.
examples: Talvella tarvitset saappaat. — In winter you need boots.
Nämä saappaat ovat vedenpitävät. — These boots are waterproof.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PU-ku'. Can mean a suit (formal) or a costume. Context determines meaning. Partitive: pukua.
examples: Hänellä on tumma puku. — He has a dark suit.
Puen puvun juhliin. — I will wear a suit to the party.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HA-me'. Simple and common word. Partitive: hametta. Plural: hameet.
examples: Tämä hame on liian lyhyt. — This skirt is too short.
Hänellä on punainen hame. — She has a red skirt.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PU-se-ro'. A lighter top, typically for women. Partitive: puseroa. Plural: puserot.
examples: Valkoinen pusero on tyylikäs. — A white blouse is stylish.
Ostan uuden puseron. — I will buy a new blouse.

items.notes: Pronounced 'NEU-le'. Refers to a knitted garment. Also 'villapaita' (wool shirt) is used for sweater. Partitive: neuletta.
examples: Tämä neule on lämmin. — This sweater is warm.
Mummo kutoi minulle neuleen. — Grandma knitted me a sweater.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VYÖ'. Short word with the Finnish ö vowel. Partitive: vyötä. Plural: vyöt.
examples: Tarvitsen uuden vyön. — I need a new belt.
Nahkavyö on kestävä. — A leather belt is durable.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SOL-mi-o'. Derived from 'solmu' (knot). Partitive: solmiota. Plural: solmiot.
examples: Hänellä on punainen solmio. — He has a red tie.
En osaa sitoa solmiota. — I cannot tie a necktie.

items.notes: Pronounced 'A-lus-VAAT-teet'. Compound: alus (under) + vaatteet (clothes). Always plural. Singular: alusvaate.
examples: Pakkaa alusvaatteet mukaan. — Pack underwear along.
Ostan puhtaat alusvaatteet. — I will buy clean underwear.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TEE-pai-ta'. Compound of the letter T + paita (shirt). Very common in casual speech. Plural: t-paidat.
examples: Kesällä käytän t-paitaa. — In summer I wear a t-shirt.
Tämä t-paita on mukava. — This t-shirt is comfortable.

items.notes: Pronounced 'FAR-kut'. Loanword, always plural in Finnish. Singular 'farkku' is not typically used. Partitive: farkkuja.
examples: Pidän sinisistä farkuista. — I like blue jeans.
Nämä farkut ovat uudet. — These jeans are new.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LENK-ka-rit'. Plural of 'lenkkari', derived from 'lenkki' (jog/loop). Casual athletic shoes.
examples: Laita lenkkarit jalkaan. — Put on your sneakers.
Ostan uudet lenkkarit juoksemista varten. — I will buy new sneakers for running.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SAN-daa-lit'. Loanword, easy to recognize. Plural of 'sandaali'.
examples: Kesällä käytän sandaaleja. — In summer I wear sandals.
Nämä sandaalit ovat mukavat. — These sandals are comfortable.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KÄ-si-neet'. Plural of 'käsine', derived from 'käsi' (hand). Mittens without separate fingers. Compare with 'hansikkaat' (fingered gloves).
examples: Lapset tarvitsevat käsineet. — The children need mittens.
Villaiset käsineet ovat lämpimät. — Wool mittens are warm.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PI-po'. Very common Finnish word, essential for winter. Partitive: pipoa. Plural: pipot.
examples: Laita pipo päähän, on kylmä! — Put a beanie on, it is cold!
Tämä pipo on villainen. — This beanie is made of wool.

items.notes: Pronounced 'LII-vi'. Can mean a formal waistcoat or a casual vest. Partitive: liiviä. Plural: liivit.
examples: Hänellä on harmaa liivi. — He/she has a grey vest.
Liivi sopii paidan päälle. — A vest goes over a shirt.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SHORT-sit'. Loanword from English, always plural. Partitive: shortseja.
examples: Kesällä käytän shortseja. — In summer I wear shorts.
Nämä shortsit ovat liian pienet. — These shorts are too small.

items.notes: Pronounced 'UI-ma-PU-ku'. Compound: uima (swimming) + puku (suit). Partitive: uimapukua. Also 'uimahousuit' for swim trunks.
examples: Ota uimapuku mukaan rannalle. — Take a swimsuit to the beach.
Tarvitsen uuden uimapuvun. — I need a new swimsuit.

items.notes: Pronounced 'SA-de-TAK-ki'. Compound: sade (rain) + takki (jacket). Very useful in Finnish weather. Partitive: sadetakkia.
examples: Ota sadetakki mukaan, sataa kohta. — Take a raincoat, it will rain soon.
Sadetakki on vedenpitävä. — The raincoat is waterproof.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TAL-vi-TAK-ki'. Compound: talvi (winter) + takki (jacket). Essential item in Finland. Partitive: talvitakkia.
examples: Talvitakki pitää sinut lämpimänä. — A winter jacket keeps you warm.
Ostan uuden talvitakin syksyllä. — I will buy a new winter jacket in autumn.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VIL-la-SUK-ka'. Compound: villa (wool) + sukka (sock). A beloved Finnish tradition. Plural: villasukat.
examples: Villasukat ovat lämpimät. — Wool socks are warm.
Mummo kutoi villasukkia. — Grandma knitted wool socks.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAA-la-ri'. Can mean work overalls or a children's snow suit (toppahaalari). Partitive: haalaria. Plural: haalarit.
examples: Lapset käyttävät haalareita talvella. — Children wear overalls in winter.
Työ haalari suojaa vaatteita. — Work overalls protect the clothes.
